Facts.text: Sea battle at Zwartewaal: William, Duke of Bavaria, defeats the forces of his mother, Margaret II, Countess of Hainaut, and her English allies
The first printing of the Zohar in Spain popularizes the Jewish study of Kabbalah
Battle of The Three Kings [Battle of Alcazar quivir): Moroccans defeat a Moroccan-Portuguese alliance in northern Morocco with 10,000 killed
Johan Maurits is appointed governor of Dutch Brazil
Hurricane hits Guadeloupe, Martinique, and St. Christopher, killing thousands
St. James' Day naval battle is an English victory against the Dutch during the Second Anglo-Dutch War off the Kent coast
Date traditionally ascribed to Dom Pérignon's invention of Champagne
The Peace of Montreal: Thirty Amerindian tribes sign an accord guaranteeing their neutrality in the event of conflict between France and England
War of the Spanish Succession: English and Dutch troops occupy Gibraltar
Crown prince Frederick of Prussia escapes to England
Jury acquits John Zenger (NY Weekly Journal) charged with seditious libel by the royal governor of NY (a victory for freedom of the press)
Retired British cavalry officer Philip Astley establishes his riding school with performances in London, a precursor to the circus
French Revolution: The National Constituent Assembly meets and issues the first decrees that abolish centuries of feudalism in France
United States Revenue Cutter Service is established to serve as an armed customs enforcement service (becoming the US Coast Guard in 1915)
The Treaty of Sistova is signed, ending the Ottoman-Habsburg wars
First edition of the American magazine "The Saturday Evening Post"
Battle of Kos is fought between Turks and Greeks
Plans for the city of Chicago are laid out
Emigrant ship Cataraqui wrecks in Bass Strait with the loss of 400 lives; only nine survive, Australia's deadliest maritime civilian disaster
The Hinomaru is established as the official flag to be flown from Japanese ships
John Bartlett publishes "Familiar Quotations," a compilation of quotations by 169 authors, including the Bible, William Shakespeare, and the great English poets
Land and naval action near Brazos Santiago, Texas
British Red Cross Society is formed
122°F (50°C) recorded in Seville, Spain (European record)
Colombia adopts a new constitution
Henry A. Rucker is appointed Collector of Internal Revenue for Georgia
An allied expeditionary force, made up of Japanese, Russian, British, French, and American troops, sets off from Tientsin for Peking, China, to put down the Boxer Rebellion
The Greenwich foot tunnel under the River Thames opens
Cardinal Giuseppe Sarto of Venice is elected Pope Pius X, known as the "Pope of the Poor and Humble"
German fleet under Admiral Wilhelm Souchon fire on the Algerian coast
US declares neutrality on the outbreak of WWI
WWI: German army shoots Belgian priests and burns down the village of Battice
WWI: King Albert I becomes Supreme Commander of Belgian army after German declaration of war
Denmark and the United States sign a treaty whereby the Danish West Indies, including the Virgin Islands, are ceded to the US in 1917 for $25 million
The Turks attack the British line at Romani in the northern Sinai (WWI)
Pravda calls for the killing of all capitalists, priests, and officers
Hungarian communist leader Béla Kun flees to Vienna after the Hungarian Soviet Republic is overthrown by the Romanian Army
Lizzie Murphy becomes the first female to play against MLB players in a charity exhibition: All-Stars from New England and the AL vs. Boston Red Sox at Fenway Park
First Dutch government of Prime Minister Hendrikus Colijn forms
US marines leave Nicaragua after 13-year occupation
Cleveland Indians, trailing 6-5 in the 9th with 2 outs, score an AL record-tying 9 runs to beat the NY Yankees 14-6
Jones Beach in New York opens to the public
Child labor laws established in Belgium
II British Empire Games open at London's White City Stadium
American Helen Stephens wins the 100 m in 11.5 seconds at the Berlin Olympics, defeating controversial intersex athlete Stanisława Walasiewicz of Poland
Greek Prime Minister Ioannis Metaxas leads a self-coup, giving him authoritarian control of the country
Nazi collaborators blow up Jewish synagogue in Zandvoort, Netherlands
Brooklyn Dodgers catcher Mickey Owen becomes the first MLB player to catch three foul pop-ups in one inning (third) in an 11-6 win against the NY Giants
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillarrives in Cairo, Egypt
German occupiers order all Dutch homing pigeons killed
The first train carrying Jews departs from Mechelen, Belgium, for Auschwitz
British premierWinston Churchilltravels to Canada on the Queen Mary
Soviet units reach the suburbs of Orel
The U.S. Army Air Forces bomb German forces in Troina
British 8th Army reaches suburbs of Florence, Italy
Red Sox outfielder Tom McBride drives in an MLB record-tying 6 runs in an inning (4th) during a 15-4 win against Washington
An earthquake of magnitude 8.0 hits northern Dominican Republic, killing 100 people and leaving 20,000 homeless
American boxer Ike Williams knocks out Bob Montgomery in six rounds in Philadelphia to unify the world lightweight title
The Supreme Court of Japan is established
An American sweep of the medals in the 110 m hurdles at the London Olympics with William Porter taking gold, ahead of Clyde Scott and Craig Dixon
Fanny Blankers-Koen of the Netherlands wins the 80 m hurdles in an Olympic record of 11.2 seconds at the London Olympics, the second of an unprecedented four individual track and field gold medals
Five-day Southern States filibuster succeeds in maintaining America's poll tax
Hungary's Olga Gyarmati wins the first ever Olympic women's long jump competition at the London Games
The women's shot put is introduced to the track and field program at the London Olympics; inaugural gold medalist Micheline Ostermeyer of France also wins the women's discus gold
Black families move into Trumbull Park housing project in Chicago
New York Yankees pitcher Vic Raschi sets an MLB record for a pitcher by driving in 7 runs in a 15-0 win against Detroit
First flight of the supersonic P-1 Lightning from Boscombe Down
The Government of Pakistan approves the National Anthem, written by Hafeez Jullundhry and composed by Ahmed G. Chagla

At a NASCAR Grand National race in Tulsa, Oklahoma, Lee Petty stops his car on lap 32, climbs the flagstand, and waves a red flag to the field, halting the race due to dusty conditions; the race is never completed
German Wilhelm Herz becomes first to ride a motorcycle over 200 mph (210 mph or 338 km/h) at the Bonneville Salt Flats, Utah
Indonesia says it will not pay debts to the Netherlands
Rocket-propelled USAF research aircraft sets record at 2,150 mph (3,460 km/h)
28th NFL Chicago College All-Star Game at Soldier Field: Philadelphia 28, All-Stars 14 (66,000 attendees)
Spokane, Washington reaches an August record of 108°F (42.22°C)
Bodies of civil rights workers Michael Schwerner, Andrew Goodman, and James E. Chaney are discovered in an earthen Mississippi dam

North Vietnam purportedly engages US Navy ships in the Gulf of Tonkin for a second time, resulting in US Congress passing the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, granting authority to assist any Southeast Asian nation facing "communist aggression"; a later investigation deems the second attack never happened as shipboard communications were misinterpreted
The Cook Islands become self-governing for the first time since 1823, governing internally with New Zealand responsible for external affairs
Eighth British Empire and Commonwealth Games open in Kingston, Jamaica; first Games held outside the "White Dominions"
34th NFL Chicago College All-Star Game at Soldier Field: Green Bay 27, All-Stars 0 (70,934 attendees)
The Association of North American Radio Clubs (ANARC) holds its first convention in Chicago
US performs a nuclear test at the Nevada Test Site
USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
WXTV TV channel 41 in New York-Paterson, NY (UNI) begins broadcasting
The US launches the first satellite into lunar orbit from a manned spacecraft
Arthur Bremer is jailed for 63 years for shooting Alabama Governor George Wallace

New York Yankees pitcher Lindy McDaniel puts in one of the best relief stints in MLB history, entering in the second inning and allowing only one run in 13 innings in a 3-2 win against the Detroit Tigers
The Crawford-Butler Act allows Puerto Ricans to elect their own governor
England beats Australia by 8 wickets in the limited-overs cricket international at Lord's; it is the first time women are permitted to play on the main square at "the home of cricket"
USSR conducts a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
US performs a nuclear test at the Nevada Test Site
US President Carter establishes the Department of Energy
USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Hurricane Aline kills 272 people in Texas and the Caribbean
Columbia is mated with SRBs and an external tank for the STS-2 mission

Outfielder Joel Youngblood is the only MLB player to get hits for two different teams in two different cities on the same day: singles for the Mets in a Chicago day game, gets traded, then singles for the Expos in a Philadelphia night game
Bettino Craxi is sworn in as Prime Minister of Italy, the first Socialist to hold the office
France performs a nuclear test
Revolution in the Republic of Upper Volta brings Thomas Sankara to power
Americans Al Joyner and Mike Conley win the gold and silver medals in the men's triple jump at the Los Angeles Olympics
Republic of Upper Volta becomes Burkina Faso (National Day)
American hang glider Larry Tudor sets a world record for gain of height, climbing 4,343 meters in one flight
Reports of a probable OPEC (Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries) agreement on output quotas send oil prices higher
USFL owners vote to suspend operations for the 1986 season and fold before the fourth season after winning only token damages in an antitrust lawsuit against the NFL
FCC votes 4-0 to rescind the Fairness Doctrine for broadcasters
Congress votes to grant $20,000 to each Japanese American interned during WWII
Hertz car rental is fined $6.85 million and agrees to pay approximately $13.7 million to customers and insurance companies in a consumer fraud case
95.5°F (35.3°C) is recorded in De Bilt, Netherlands (highest August temperature in the Netherlands)
European Community proposes a boycott of Iraq
The Greek cruise ship Oceanos sinks off the Wild Coast of South Africa
In its debut at the Barcelona Olympics the badminton singles gold medals are both won by Indonesian athletes, whilst both men's and women's doubles go to South Korea
Italian fencer Giovanna Trillini wraps up gold medals in both women's foil events at the Barcelona Olympics as part of the winning team
The diving competition at the Barcelona Olympics concludes with Chinese athletes dominating, winning three of the four gold medals contested; American Mark Lenzi wins the 3 m springboard
Angolan air force bombs Huambo
Rwandan Hutus and Tutsis sign a peace treaty in Arusha, Tanzania
Galaxy Dwingeloo 1 near the Milky Way is discovered
Truck carrying 24 million honeybees overturns on the New York Thruway near Tappan Zee Bridge
XXVI Summer Olympic Games close at Centennial Olympic Stadium in Atlanta, Georgia
"The Keenan Ivory Wayans Show" premieres on Fox TV
185,000 Teamsters Union United Parcel Service drivers walk off their jobs
In Colombia, international oil company Occidental Petroleum declares force majeure on all oil exports from the Cano Limon field after a series of attacks knock out a major oil pipeline
Previous Bolivian dictator, Hugo Banzer, wins second presidency term through democratic process
Soham murders: 10-year-old school girls Jessica Chapman and Holly Wells go missing from the town of Soham, Cambridgeshire
Dame Silvia Cartwright steps down as the Governor-General of New Zealand and is replaced by The Honourable Anand Satyanand, who is sworn in on 23 August
NASA's Phoenix spaceship is launched

On the third anniversary of his 500th MLB career home run,Alex Rodriguezreaches the 600 HR milestone in the NY Yankees' 5-1 win against Toronto; he is the seventh and youngest player to achieve this milestone
Mark Duggan is shot by police in London at 29, sparking widespread riots that cause an estimated £200 million in property damage
A suicide bombing in Abyan, Yemen, kills 45 people
South African Oscar Pistorius becomes the first double-leg amputee to compete at the Olympics in the 400 m event in London; he is out in the semifinals
Super Rugby Final, Waikato Stadium, Hamilton: Chiefs win their first Super Rugby title with a 37-6 victory over the Sharks (Durban, RSA) at home
Hamas and Israel agree to another 72-hour ceasefire
A plague of locusts in southern Russia prompts a state of emergency to be declared
Floods partially sweep two passenger trains off a bridge in Madhya Pradesh, India, killing 31 people and injuring 100
Muppets Miss Piggy and Kermit the Frog announce the end of their relationship on Twitter
Aldi supermarket withdraws all eggs from sale after the chemical fipronil is found in eggs from the Netherlands
Super Rugby Final, AMI Stadium, Christchurch: Canterbury Crusaders successfully defend their title with a 37-18 home win against the Lions from Johannesburg; Crusaders' 9th Super Rugby title

Australian cricket batsman Steve Smith hits a brilliant 142 following his first innings of 144 in the first Test against England at Edgbaston; he is only the fourth batsman of all time to score more than 140 in each innings of a Test match
Car explosion kills 20 and injures 47 in Cairo, Egypt, in a terrorist-related incident
China's central bank allows its currency to weaken under usual 7-1 ratio to the US dollar for first time in a decade in response to further US trade tariffs
Franky Zapata becomes the first person to cross the English Channel by flyboard in 22 minutes
Gunman kills 9 and injures 27 at a bar in Dayton, Ohio, in the second mass shooting in 24 hours in the US
COVID-19 infection spike forces a return to lockdown in Manila and surrounding provinces in the Philippines, affecting 27 million people as cases surpass 100,000
Huge explosions at the port of Beirut, Lebanon, kill more than 200 and leave over 6,000 people injured
UN says COVID-19 pandemic creates the biggest educational disruption in history, affecting nearly 1.6 billion students in 190 countries, 94% worldwide
Americans Sydney McLaughlin and Dalilah Muhammad go 1-2 in the women's 400 m hurdles at the Tokyo Olympics; McLaughlin sets a world record of 51.46 seconds
Globally known COVID-19 cases surpass 200 million, with the death toll at 4.2 million, according to Johns Hopkins
Mediterranean region evolves into a "wildfire hotspot" according to authorities amidst extreme temperatures, with Greece experiencing one of its worst heatwaves in 40 years
The Dixie Fire at 320,000 acres, the largest wildfire in California so far in 2021, destroys the Gold Rush town of Greenville
A Russian court sentences WNBA star Brittney Griner to nine years in prison for drug smuggling amid claims she is being used as a pawn between the US and Russia
Mudslide in the Chinese city of Xi’an kills at least 21 people following Typhoon Khanun
At the Paris Olympics, Noah Lyles becomes the first American sprinter to win the 100 m gold medal in 20 years; his time of 9.784 seconds edges Jamaica's Kishane Thompson by 0.005 seconds

Double American world records at the Paris Olympics with swimmer Bobby Finke setting 14:30.67 in the men's 1,500 m freestyle and the 4 x 100 m medley relay team of Regan Smith, Lilly King, Gretchen Walsh, and Torri Huske setting 3:49.63
Swedish swim star Sarah Sjöström wins the 50 m freestyle in 23.71 seconds to complete the 50/100 m sprint double at the Paris Olympics
Battle of Evesham: English Prince Edward (laterEdward I) defeats baronial forces of Simon de Montfort, who is killed and his body mutilated
English troops underEdward IIIconquer Calais, becomes strategic town for the English during Hundred Years' War
London's Lord Mayor repossesses the Steelyard from the Hanze (German Hanseatic League) on instruction fromElizabeth I
21-year-oldGeorge Washingtonbecomes a master mason
English poet and artistWilliam Blake, aged 14, is first apprenticed to engraver James Basire in London

Indian Wars: While protecting a railroad survey party in Montana, the US 7th Cavalry, under Lieutenant ColonelGeorge Armstrong Custer, clashes for the first time with the Sioux near the Tongue River, killing one man on each side
PopeLeo XIIIpublishes encyclical Aeterni Patris
Queen Wilhelminaand Queen Emma open the Merwede Canal between Amsterdam and the Rhine
Sunday school teacherLizzie Borden's father and stepmother are murdered with an axe in Fall River, Massachusetts; Borden is later arrested, tried, and acquitted
MLB pitching duel: Philadelphia A's Jack Coombs and White SoxEd Walshpitch a 0-0 tie in 16 innings
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonissues his "Proclamation of Neutrality" to keep the United States out of the war in Europe
Adolf Hitlerreceives the Iron Cross First Class for bravery on the recommendation of his Jewish superior, Lieutenant Hugo Gutmann
RodinMuseum opens in Paris in the Hôtel Biron, containing works left to the state by the sculptorAuguste Rodin
Giants outfielderMel Ottbecomes the first player in MLB history to score six runs in a game as New York beats Phillies 21-4
American athleteJesse Owenswins his second gold medal at the Berlin Olympics, defeating German Luz Long in the long jump final with an Olympic record
Winston Churchilldeparts on the Prince of Wales to the US
Colonel general Jeremenko arrives in Stalingrad and is welcomed byNikita Khrushchev
Anne Frankis arrested in Amsterdam by German Security Police (Grüne Polizei) following a tip-off from an informer who is never identified
Finnish government passes a special act makingCarl Gustaf Emil Mannerheimpresident of Finland
Gambling boss Theodore Roe is murdered by the crew ofSam Giancana
Eisenhowerauthorizes $46 million for the construction of CIA headquarters
Argentine Maserati driverJuan Manuel Fangioclinches his record fifth F1 World Drivers' Championship (fourth consecutive) by winning the German Grand Prix at the Nürburgring
Billboard Hot 100 is published for the first time with "Poor Little Fool" byRicky Nelsonat #1
Nelson Mandelais captured by South African police
Pink Floydreleases their first album "The Piper at The Gates of Dawn," their only record made under the leadership ofSyd Barrett
Jim Morrisonarrested for public drunkenness in Los Angeles
Ugandan dictatorIdi Aminorders the expulsion of 50,000 Asians with British passports from Uganda
Led Zeppelin singerRobert Plantis seriously injured in a car crash in Rhodes, Greece
Seven-time All-StarMaury Willsbecomes only the third Black manager in MLB history when Darrell Johnson is fired by the Seattle Mariners after a nine-game losing streak
Oliver Northis assigned to White House duty
Danielle Steel’s romance novel “Thurston House” is published
American athleteCarl Lewiswins the 100 m in 9.9 seconds in Los Angeles, the first of nine Olympic gold medals over three Games
Prince's "Purple Rain" album goes to #1 and stays at #1 for 24 weeks
Future Hall of Fame infielderRod Carewof the California Angels singles off Minnesota's Frank Viola to collect his 3,000th career MLB hit; the Angels win 6–5
New York Yankees retire Baseball Hall of Fame shortstopPhil Rizzuto's #10
Tom Seaverof Chicago White Sox becomes 17th pitcher to win 300 MLB career games, defeating New York 4-1 at Yankee Stadium (54,032 attendees)
Tony Gwynngets six hits in an 11-10 win against the Giants, the fourth time this season the Padre has five or more hits, tying the MLB record
Howard Sterndrops out of NY gubernatorial race
Operation Storm begins in Croatia as the Croatian army attacks territory under the Republic of Serbian Krajina in the largest land battle since WWII
Jim Bunning,Earl Weaver, Bill Foster, and Ned Hanlon are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame, Cooperstown, NY
William Ryan Holt and Michael Mischler are arrested for breaking intoElle Macpherson’s Los Angeles home

Australian cricket spinnerShane Warneskittles England (162) with 6/33 to guide tourists to a 7-wicket third Test victory at Trent Bridge; Aussies regain the Ashes with a record seventh straight Test win against England
American Hall of Fame jockeyPat Day(52) announces his retirement from the saddle with $298 million in career purses
Prime MinisterPaul Martinannounces that Michaëlle Jean will be Canada's 27th and first Black Governor General
32-year-oldAlex Rodriguezbecomes the youngest player in MLB history to hit 500 home runs, connecting on the first pitch he sees as the NY Yankees beat Kansas City 16-8
San Francisco sluggerBarry Bondshomers off Clay Hensley in the second inning of a 3-2 loss to San Diego to tieHank Aaron’s 33-year-old MLB career home run record of 755
Kim Jong-ilmeets former PresidentBill Clintonand pardons and releases captured American journalists Euna Lee and Laura Ling, who were found guilty of entering the country illegally
California's Proposition 8, the ballot initiative prohibiting same-sex marriage passed by the state's voters in 2008, is overturned by Judge Vaughn Walker in the case Perry v.Schwarzenegger

Golden double for the US at the London Olympic tennis asSerena WilliamsdefeatsMaria Sharapova6-0, 6-1 in women's singles; Bob and Mike Bryan win men’s doubles, beating the French pair Michael Llodra and Jo-Wilfried Tsonga 6-4, 7-6
The US wins the 4 x 100 m medley relay double at the London Olympics withMichael Phelpscollecting his 18th Olympic gold medal; the women's team sets a world record of 3:52.05
Hassan Rouhaniis inaugurated as the President of Iran
Forbes Magazine namesRobert Downey Jr. the world's highest-paid actor with earnings of $80 million; four non-Hollywood actors make the top 10
Rwandan PresidentPaul Kagamewins a third term with 98.63% of the vote
PresidentNicolás Maduroof Venezuela survives an assassination attempt by drone live on TV
Rihannais named the world's wealthiest female musician, worth $1.7 billion by Forbes
Infowars conspiracy theoristAlex Jonesis ordered to pay $4.1 million in compensatory damages to parents of a child killed in the Sandy Hook massacre after Jones claimed it was a hoax
US Attorney GeneralMerrick Garlandannounces charges against four police officers for violating the rights ofBreonna Taylorduring a 2020 raid that resulted in her death
UK PMKeir Starmer, in a televised address, condemns the escalating right-wing violence around the country aimed at immigrants and ethnic groups, with over 150 people arrested
Russian Antarctic expedition led byFabian Gottlieb von Bellingshausenreturns to Kronstadt after being the first to circumnavigate Antarctica
WWI: Germany declares war on Belgium; Britain declares war on Germany
Richard de Clare, 6th Earl of Gloucester, 5th Earl of Hertford, 2nd Lord of Glamorgan, and 8th Lord of Clare, born in Suffolk, Kingdom of England (d. 1262)
Külüg Khan, Chinese Prince of Huaining, 3rd Emperor of the Yuan Dynasty, born in Khanbaliq, Yuan China (d. 1311)
Leopold I, "The Glorious", Duke of Austria and Styria, member of the House of Habsburg, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1326)
Pieter Coecke van Aelst, Flemish artist, architect and designer of tapestries and stained glass (court painter to Holy Roman Emperor Charles V), born in Aelst (d. 1550)
Pope Urban VII, Roman head of the Catholic Church and shortest recognized ruler of the Papal States, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1590)
François Hédelin, abbé d'Aubignac, French writer, born in Paris (d. 1676)
Louis Lully, French musician and son of Jean-Baptiste Lully, born in Paris, France (d. 1734)
Thomas Blackwell, Scottish classical scholar, born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1757)
Václav Matyáš Gurecký, Bohemian composer, born in Přerov, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1743)
Johann Gottlob Lehmann, German minerologist and geologist, born in Bad Gottleuba-Berggießhübel, Germany (d. 1767)
Granville Leveson-Gower, 1st Marquess of Stafford, Viscount Trentham, English politician in the House of Lords, born in England (d. 1803)
Giuseppe Colla, Italian composer, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (d. 1806)
Maximilian Stadler, Austrian pianist and composer and Benedictine monk, born in Melk, Austria (d. 1833)
Nicolas-Jacque Conte, French painter and inventor (modern pencil), born in Aunou-sur-Orne, France (d. 1805)
Wenzel Sedlak, Bohemian clarinetist and composer, born in Jezbořice, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1851)
Olof Wilhelm Uddén, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1868)
William Rowan Hamilton, Irish astronomer and mathematician, born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1865)
Carl Reinhold August Wunderlich, German physician (established normal temperature as  37 °C (98.6 °F)), born in Sulz am Neckar (d. 1877)
Israel Vogdes, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Chester County, Pennsylvania (d. 1889)
Lovell Harrison Rosseau, American lawyer politician and Major General (Union Army), born in Stanford, Kentucky (d. 1869)
James Springer White, American theologian (co-founder of the Seventh-day Adventist Church), born in Palmyra, Maine (d. 1881)
Oliver Hazard Perry Throck Morton, American politician (Governor of Indiana), born in Salisbury, Indiana (d. 1877)
Arthur Haygarth, English cricket batsman (Middlesex & Surrey, cricket historian), born in Hastings, Sussex, England (d. 1903)
John Venn, English mathematician (Venn Diagram), born in Kingston upon Hull, Yorkshire, England (d. 1923)
Walter Pater, English aesthete and writer (Plato & Platonism), born in London (d. 1894)
Florimond Van Duyse, Belgian composer and musicologist, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1910)
Silas Gamaliel Pratt, American composer (The Tragedy of the Deep), born in Addison, Vermont (d. 1916)
Knut Hamsun, Norwegian writer (Nobel Prize in Literature 1920), born in Lom, Gudbrandsdalen, Norway (d. 1952)
Gus Kempis, South African cricketer (South Africa's 1st Test), born in Port Elizabeth, South Africa (d. 1890)

Jake Beckley, American Baseball HOF first baseman (23,767 putouts MLB record; career .308 hitter; Pittsburgh Alleghenys/Pirates; NY Giants; Cincinnati Reds; St. Louis Cardinals), born in Hannibal, Missouri (d. 1918)
Harry Lauder, Scottish comedian and singer (Roamin' in the Gloamin), born in Portobello, Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1950)
Damaso Berenguer, Spanish soldier and politician (122nd Prime Minister of Spain), born in San Juan de los Remedios, Cuba (d. 1953)
Dame Laura Knight, English impressionist artist, born in Long Eaton, Derbyshire, England (d. 1970)
René Schickele, German-French writer, sometimes under nom de plume 'Sascha' (Erbe am Rhein), poet, and magazine editor (Weissen Blätter), born in Obernai, Alsace-Lorraine, German Empire (d. 1940)
Henri Cornet, French cyclist who won the second Tour de France, born in Desvres, France (d. 1941)
Philip Greeley Clapp, American composer and educator (University of Iowa, 1919-54), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1954)
Syedna Taher Saifuddin, Bohra Spiritual Leader, born in Surat, India (d. 1965)
Dolf Luque, Cuban Baseball HOF pitcher (World Series 1919, 33; MLB wins leader & MLB ERA leader 1923; Cincinnati Reds, NY Giants), born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1957)
Erich Weinert, German communist writer, born in Magdeburg (d. 1953)
Johanna Bordewijk-Roepman, Dutch composer, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1971)
Joseph Calleia, Maltese-American stage, screen, and radio actor (The Jungle Book; Gilda; Touch of Evil), born in Mdina, Malta (d. 1975)
Ernesto Maserati, Italian auto racer and engineer (director, chief engineer Maserati of Modena), born in Bologna, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1975)
Ezra Taft Benson, 13th President of Latter-day Saints and US Secretary of Agriculture, born in Whitney, Idaho (d. 1994)
Arturo Umberto Illia, President of Argentina (1963-66), born in Pergamino, Argentina (d. 1983)
Douglas L. Mays, English cartoonist (Punch), born in Kingston-upon-Thames, England (d. 1991)
Nabi Tajima, 2nd oldest Japanese supercentenarian and the last living person certified to have been born in the 19th century, born in Kikaijima, Japan (d. 2018)
Clarence Passailaigue, Jamaican cricketer (487 for 6th wkt for Jamaica), born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 1972)
Clara Peller, Russian-American manicurist and commercial spokesperson (Wendy's - "Where's the beef?"), born in Imperial Russia (d. 1987)
Wild Bill Hallahan, American baseball player, born in Binghamton, New York (d. 1981)
Tony Gulotta, American auto racer (Indianapolis 500, 1927 3rd), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1981)
Helen Kane [Schroeder], American actress (Heads Up, Pointed Heels), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 1966)
Joe Tate, English footballer, born in Old Hill, Cradley Heath, England (d. 1973)
Theodore Newton, American actor (Ace of Aces, Voltaire), born in Lawrenceville, New Jersey (d. 1963)
Witold Gombrowicz, Polish author (Ferdydurke, Pornography), born in Małoszyce, Russian Empire (d. 1969)
Boris Alexandrov, Soviet-Russian conductor (Alexandrov Ensemble, 1946-86), and composer, chiefly of patriotic and military music, born in Bologoye, Russian Empire (d. 1994)
Michael Scott Montague Fordham, English child psychiatrist and Jungian analyst, born in Kensington, London (d. 1995)
Marie-Jose von Saksen-Coburg, Belgian Princess (last Queen of Italy), born in Ostend, Belgium (d. 2001)
Kurt Eichhorn, German conductor, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1994)
Osbert Lancaster, English cartoonist and author (Daily Express), born in London (d. 1986)
Roberto Burle Marx, Brazilian landscape gardener, born in São Paulo, Brazil (d. 1994)
Saunders Mac Lane, American mathematician (co-founded category theory), born in Taftville, Connecticut (d. 2005)
(Floyd) "Stumpy" Brady, American jazz trombonist (Andy Kirk, Teddy Wilson, Luckey Roberts), born in Brownsville, Pennsylvania (d. 1998)
Anita Page, American silent screen actress (Our Dancing Daughters), born in Flushing, Queens (d. 2008)
Hedda Sterne, Romanian born American artist, born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 2011)
William Schuman, American composer (Pulitzer Prize, 1943), and administrator (Juilliard, 1945-61; Lincoln Center, 1961-68), born in New York City (d. 1992)
Aleksandr Danilovich Aleksandrov, Russian mathematician, physicist, philosopher, and mountaineer, born in Volyn, Ryazan Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1999)
David Raksin, American composer renown for his work in film and TV (Laura; Modern Times), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2004)
Raoul Wallenberg, Swedish diplomat in WW II (saved 10,000s of Jews), born in Lidingö Municipality, Sweden (d. 1947)
Robert Hayden, African-American poet (Those Winter Sundays), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1980)
Wesley Addy, American actor (Kiss Me Deadly, Bostonians, Loving), born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1996)
Irving Fields [Yitzhak Schwartz], American lounge pianist and composer (Ilona Massey Show; Bagels & Bongos), born in New York City (d. 2016)
Warren Avis, American entrepreneur (Avis Rent a Car System), born in Bay City, Michigan (d. 2007)
Iceberg Slim (a.k.a. Robert Beck), African-American author, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1992)
Helen Thomas, American reporter and UPI journalist (starts press conferences), born in Winchester, Kentucky (d. 2013)
Herb Ellis, American jazz guitarist (Jimmy Dorsey Orchestra, Oscar Peterson Trio, Ella Fitzgerald, Verve Records), born in Farmersville, Texas (d. 2010)
